sentences.text: We were still in the town in [INAUDIBLE] until the episode with the-- [?
[LAUGHING] ?] it's not Green Peace.
It's-- skipped my memories.
Yes.
String beans.
And this is the one what left all the way, to show them whoever took it, whoever went with them.
All the way.
And we jumped down.

Actually, we were hiding at that time for two, three days, all over the [INAUDIBLE] on the floors, me and my brother, until we united back with Mother, and left town completely, to another town by the name Otrotz.
O-T-R-O-C-Z, or T-Z.
Otrotz.
This was a town with mixed population-- half Russian, half Polish.
The reason we went to this town is we know somebody over there.
And we know that the Russian underground is coming off to visit in this town.
And we want to join them.
And that's the reason we went to this town.
This was, if I am recalling correctly, around February, March of 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we went with a family by the name of Michalik.
M-I-C-H-A-L-I-K.
They kept us for a while on the top of the barn, also.
Gave us food.
Amazing-- it was the best food what we ever got being in hiding by this family.
But they cannot-- they were afraid to keep us long.
They said that the partisans [INAUDIBLE] coming in are seldom coming to town.
If they come, they stay a day, but mostly they are in the woods not far from there.
So we went to the woods.
And at that time, my sisters went before a couple days to find out what [INAUDIBLE].
Me and my brother and mother went.
And it was March, 1944, if I remember correctly.
We walked in the woods on a straight road.
And it was a road coming in, like, a 45 from this side.
And [INAUDIBLE] we could not see who was coming from this side until we come head-on.
And it was a patrol from three Germans with their rifles like this.
And we come close.
So each one run, and they start shooting.
We saw mother was falling down.
It was down from a mountain.
She fell.
Me and my brother escaped at that time.
And we went far in the woods.
And that time, we were hearing the tanks with artillery pieces from the German all over.
They were coming into the woods to look for the underground.
And we did not know where to hide.
So I was a good climber on trees.
So I went first on a big tree on the top.
[POLISH] they call it in Polish-- very huge one.
I picked a place where my brother could not see me.
I switched to another place for him until we picked two places what it's difficult to see from the bottom.
He climbed up, and we were sitting a whole day on the trees.
From the bottom we looked down.
The Germans [INAUDIBLE].
And when they went, late in the evening, it was quiet.
They left, but they were afraid in the night to be in the woods-- the Germans.
That time, we were not afraid to walk around.
So we climbed down from the-- from the tree.
As we climbed down, we walked-- I stepped in-- like, I fell down with my foot in a hole.
We moved away the branches-- was an opening.
We did not know how deep this opening is.
So we took a long branch, and we put it down, and we measured the size-- like, we could estimate now 10 feet, the depth.
We didn't know what is there and how far, so we were afraid in the night to go down.
So we were sleeping outside.
In daytime, it gets light.
I jumped down.
After I jumped down, I told my brother, it's maybe 4 by 4.
I think it's a nature-made place, but I doubt if somebody dug this.
And he put in a big branch, in, like, using it as a ladder, and prepared some branches to cover up the hole.
And we were sitting three days over there in this hole.
After three days, I went out, the first one to go looking for food.
I was afraid to go into the town.
I found in the field [INAUDIBLE] the farmers are leaving potatoes in the field in a position like pyramid.
Potatoes they cover around with straw and then with dirt.
So with a knife, I was cutting through the dirt and pulling out raw potatoes.
And this is what I brought back, and we were eating it.
I have to admit, it tasted better at that time than the best steak today.
[LAUGHS]
And after three or four days, we came back to town.
We found out that mother's alive.
When she fell down, she fell next to a bunker where underground people were hiding.
And they heard the shooting.
And when the Germans left, they took her in, inside.
They were with another group.
When they went to the woods before, they met a group hiding already semi-underground.
They had weapons with them, and they were waiting to join underground, but some of them were killed from Polish underground.
They called them [POLISH] in Polish.
[POLISH]-- this is like national guard, [? like they ?] call them here.
And most of them-- not quite most of them.
The one that survived in '44, they were in the underground in the bunkers.
And this mountain over there in the woods-- almost all of them survived.
Mother was coming looking for us.
And we were found by the same family, Michalik.
She came she said they asked if they didn't hear nothing from us.
They said no.
So they let her wait over there.
And we came looking for her also over there.
And from there, we went back to the woods, and we joined in to be in the bunkers with these people what they have already weapons, and they were going out in the night to getting food.
Yes.
Last week I met one at a bar mitzvah in Florida.
His name is [?
Lucien ?] Zukr.
Zukr would be Z-U-K-R.
And at this moment, I don't know, maybe one or two is still alive, based on the age and everything.
We were in contact all the time.
There's another gentlemen in-- no-- yeah, in New York, Manhattan.
He has a delicatessen.
His name is Leon Brener.
He was supplying us with food.
Yeah.
Brener is B-R-E-N-E-R.
Actually, he caught on with me in the woods.
When I walked, he was on a horse with a machine gun.
And this first question when he [? called down ?] [INAUDIBLE], asking me [POLISH].
[POLISH] was the security code, like one Jew to the other one that's from our nation, [POLISH].
And he asked us where we are.
He said he is in the Polish underground.
They don't know he's Jewish.
He didn't look to be Jewish.
But he is the provider [? foot ?] from the underground, and he will leave us here [INAUDIBLE].
And he is alive, and we're in contact with him.
In the woods, the life was actually much easier like the life in the bunkers.
At least you were [? considered ?] free at that situation.
You know that you will not be-- nobody will catch you to be killed.
We already had the weapons.
Like, I was reminded last week from this guy [INAUDIBLE] Zukr that the rifle what I carried was longer than me.
I was the baby, they called me.
They were all older ones.
And he is the one what came back from another underground area, where there were 20 boys. 19 was killed.
He's the only one escaped from the Polish underground.
And he escaped for one reason-- the rifle got jammed.
19 boys in 1944 what they were fighting against the Germans, together with the Polish underground.
In the end, they felt it was close to the end of the world.
They killed them, too.
Yeah.
So I considered myself-- the woods were easier, like being in the dark bunker setting.
At least you can go out, find some strawberries, mushrooms, carrots in the field.
When they grow, when they're frozen, leftover from the winter.
There was more food to put together.
And we were-- water was no problem.
Wintertime is snow, and summertime you find some places or whatever is leftover from winter.
Needless to say, it wasn't clean.
Daytime mostly was-- the majority of the day was time to sleep, and the night was time to cook.
We were cooking in the night, but we were making fire in the bunker.
You cannot see smoke in the nighttime, and as long as it was done inside.
So the nighttime, we were cooking.
The nighttime was to go out and to organize food.
And whoever wants to sleep will sleep.
But daytime was to be awake.
We had to be very well ready daytime.
We don't know who can come in and who it is.
The bunkers was-- if you figure out, maybe the length was about 15 feet.
The width, we could say about-- if we were sleeping, there was still passage by, about 8 feet.
Yeah.

They dug it, and they put some-- actually, what they did is they did not dug it out and make a roof, they dug under a mountain to leave nothing touched from the top-- just the entrance, which was closed in with bushes.
And the dirt was slowly removed out from inside.
And it was very hard work to build.
And we always built additional bunkers to have reserve, but they were not in use in case it has to be switched, more or less a mile away, one from the other.
Shovels.
Shovels was easy to organize.
You go to the farms.
They had plenty of shovels outside.
You took it.
[INAUDIBLE].
At least they don't leave a trail-- a trail.
[LAUGHS] You learn how to survive, looking for all kind of means how.
And a person [? can't ?] survive without imagination.
We were close to 60 at that time.
And they were a group of several thousand-- the beginning of the hiding here.
So if you imagine the percentage of survival.
Or in general, in Poland, there was 3 and 1/2 million Jews before the war.
Actually, survivors from the bunkers was less than 1%, excluding ghettos or Russia, where they came back or they escaped.
Yes.
They changed.
They joined the underground.
They left.
Some of them came new ones.
I went to the partisans.
There was many days they sent me out to find out if the Germans were in this town or that town.
Mixed.
No.
I think I was the youngest, and the only one in this age.
14 I was by the end of the war.
[INAUDIBLE] from 12 to 14, started the hiding.
Two years.
So in the woods-- in the woods how old were you?
Almost 14.
That's already grown up.
It's difficult to remember all the details from every day, what a person had to go through.
When I was leaving, many times, the woods to go to the town, you have to make yourself marks to come back.
And it's in the night, and you're only 14 years or 13 years old.
And in the nighttime, to find a bunker and make sure that nobody follows you, you have to put little stones by a tree.
If you find this tree, you know that to the right 100 feet, you have to find another stone.
What you put it away over there.
Then you go left so much.
Everything was calculated to know the place to find it.
Otherwise, it's impossible.
No.
This was survival.
I don't know-- you just learn by yourself, the tricks.
This was nobody to teach you.
Yes, many times.
Actually, the big rifle was in the end.
Before this, I had a short rifle.
But they cut off the wood from one side, taken away from the rifle, and cut off the pipe, just leave it short.
The only thing what this does is when you shoot, it makes such a noise like a hand grenade.
It's unbelievable.
But it's not accurate.
If you want to shoot straight, you better shoot left or right.
You'll find the person straight.
But the big rifle is more accurate.
But I have to admit, whatever time I was in the underground, I had the opportunity and the satisfaction to take-- how do they call it in English?
In Hebrew, it would be [HEBREW].
This is to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get even with Germans.
And I know from what I can verify is at least eight.
At my age, it was enough.
Without verification, I don't know how many.
But the rifle shoot not where I want to hit, so I don't know the short one.
I was very good with hand grenades, and that's maybe part of it.
But later, I was in the Israeli commandos.
They picked all the boys [INAUDIBLE] went underground.
They were bought in the beginning.
And later, it was taken by force from other people what they didn't want it.
[INAUDIBLE] they were killing Jews, the underground-- I wasn't involved with it, but I know the way.
They went with weapons, and they were threatening them.
They got the weapons from them, in our area.
I don't know how it was in others.
Later, when the Russian partisans came in, we had drops from Russia with parachutes.
So that was already-- that's how the big rifle came in.
That's right.
No.
They was-- they went out in fields where they know where the drop is.
They did not disclose exactly the places.
When they brought back the-- even food with the ammunition and medicine and everything.
Yes.
Always radio communication.
No.
This was the partisan, but they were longer [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't stay in one place.
But when I came into the headquarters, there were always the radio transmission to Russia going back and forth.
And between one group to the other.
But this-- everything happened the last two, three months-- by the end of the war in our area.
Unfortunately, this was not in the beginning.
You just get hard, like a rock, and you learn to survive.
And amazing that I was never sick at that time.
Needless to say, it's very cold.
I didn't have shoes like I have today.
I had cloth under my feet, like you take a rag, you put around with rope.
Once ?] is good nobody hear when you walk.
It's very quiet.
This was such a wonderful feeling.
When I could take [INAUDIBLE] actually to settle the scores, like they say, as much as I could.
I cannot express the feeling.
But I say, these big heroes, for they were killing our people so much, some of them was on their knees begging-- the Germans-- not to kill them.
The ones that were caught.
Unfortunately, in the partisans, there were policies not to keep any POWs.
There's no place to hold them.
But they were begging.
And you would not believe that this is the people that can burn other people.
Maybe they were between them, also, these people.
They were not all bad, the Germans.
I had encountered with Germans before we had judenrein.
The plain Wehrmacht people that were going to the Russian front-- they did not want to go over there.
They had families and everything.
Even the Germans, they were human.
But the rest, particularly the brown shells that they were wearing, the SS-- they were the biggest murderers.
Yeah.
We had a chance to talk to people what they escaped [?
Majdanek. ?] People that escaped the Warsaw ghetto.
They were for months going from woods to woods.
And until we encountered with them, we knew all the stories what's going on.
But there's no place to survive.
Everybody was telling about the ovens, what they're doing with the gas thing.
People that escaped were walking at the ovens.
[INAUDIBLE] people get undressed and going into a room, they're taking a shower, and they get a shower from gas.
And then they go in the ovens.
So we knew the whole what's going on.
It was unbelievable that nobody-- nobody from all the big nations, the free countries-- nobody said nothing, nobody did nothing.
We felt we are the forgotten people completely.
Nobody cares about us.
And maybe for this, but was no state of Israel.
There was nobody to talk in our name.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They could have stopped the Germans from burning so much people.
Just threatening to eliminate Berlin would be good enough.
At the same woods in Otrotz.
Actually, they called it [POLISH].
This was the mountain 'bull-headed,' so it was in Polish, [POLISH].
When the Germans were going back from the Russian front.
And the partisans were telling us every time which area they already liberated, how far they are.
So we knew already where they are.
And the town people came all to the woods.
They were afraid to be in town.
And they were mostly Russian people, what I mentioned, living there.
Even the other one-- this town happened to be they didn't kill nobody.
So they came to the woods, and they offered us food, and we were sitting with them together until the news came that the Germans already bypassed us.
And the Russians came into town.
And after a couple of days, we went to a city with the name Krasnik.
K-R-A-S-N-I-K.
And in this city was a small Jewish community coming to life from all the woods, from the bunkers, the survivors that we met, everybody over there.
But in the city, we felt that it's not safe to be any longer to after the war.
Three of my wives-- she wasn't my wife at that time.
My girlfriend's cousins went back to town where my [? wife ?] was hiding.
And on the way back from town to Krasnik, they all were killed, after the war.
The polish people.
Chaszow.
C-H-A-S-Z-O-W.
This is seven kilometers from the town where I was hiding, [INAUDIBLE].
Over, and we were still hiding.
We were still afraid to move.
Another friend went by train to Krakow, which was liberated.
There was a pogrom in a city by the name Kielce in Poland.
She was killed by stoning her.
And we were afraid to move by train, even after the war.
The Kielce pogrom is very well known.
It was written up all over.
And I know this-- she was hiding with us in the woods, this person.
Got killed after the war.
We knew that is the same people what they killed so much of us before.
Did not change.
And one day when the Russian came in, they want to kill us much more not to leave witnesses.
And this was the name of the game.
No.
Not this one, I have other one.
I told myself never to be without.
I have three now.
But not the weapons from over there.
It's illegal ?] here.
[LAUGHS]
After this experience in Krasnik, in Genoa, 1945, I went to Lublin.
I joined Kibbutz [?
Shanovatsayir ?], what that time already assembled.
Lublin is 50 kilometer from Krasnik.
I went by horse and wagon with a farmer.
I was afraid to go by the train.
Lublin was already more secured with bigger Russian soldiers were more in the city.
Krasnik was too little for this.
Over there, on a kibbutz, maybe a month.
In Lublin.
And as we got organized, we got false papers as Greek immigrants.
And we went to Czechoslovakia, then to Austria, and then to Italy.
